what_it_does: This noticeboard item argues that annotation intent should be split into three distinct categories—comment, suggestion, and change-request—and that the intent value should change the downstream contract of an envelope, especially by making change-requests produce a proposed diff with an accept/reject surface.
observations: It cites Google Docs Suggesting mode, GitHub suggested-change blocks, and Figma/Notion comment/action-item patterns as mature precedents.
It maps those precedents onto an envelope intent field.
It proposes change-request should yield a diff against the resolved code:// or board:// source and a one-tap Accept/Reject action.
It contrasts question/idea as thread-only and bug as carrying auto-screenshot plus computed element state.
It warns that without the split every annotation becomes an ambiguous do-something request.
It references company-specific terms such as envelope, mark-is-truth, code://, board://, and git-revert safety.
standouts: The claim that intent should change the envelope's downstream contract, not just its visual tag.
The proposal to close the comment-to-edit loop with a verifiable, reversible, one-tap accept/reject diff.
The framing of this distinction as the single most important product distinction in the category.
conventions: Follows the channel-memory item frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, and history.
Uses markdown headings, bold emphasis, and bulleted lists.
Supports its argument with analogies from well-known external tools.
Uses internal project terminology without defining it inline.
concerns: The proposal assumes the reader already understands envelope, BD-D ADDITIONS, and mark-is-truth concepts.
It does not specify the format or transport for the proposed diff.
The bug (UI mode) mention is introduced but not elaborated.
It is prescriptive product design rather than a recorded decision or implementation.
notes: A product-design note pressing for a hard intent taxonomy so annotations either spawn discussion or produce actionable, reversible diffs.
questions: What is the exact schema of the envelope and its intent field?
What does BD-D ADDITIONS refer to?
How is a diff against a resolved code:// or board:// source represented and applied?
Who is Tim and what is the brief being referenced?
What is the intended behavior when a change-request is rejected?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to name and argue for a comment/suggestion/change-request split; the content does exactly that, with examples and a downstream-contract proposal, so they match.
intention_signals: It is trying to persuade the design to treat annotation intent as a behavioral switch that turns a comment into either a discussion thread or a concrete, machine-actionable change proposal.
novelty: It reframes familiar Google Docs/GitHub suggestion mechanics into an envelope-intent contract tied to the company's git-revert safety posture.
contribution: It contributes the insight that annotation intent should drive machine-readable downstream behavior—diff generation and accept/reject surfaces—rather than acting as a cosmetic tag.
summary_for_embedding: Noticeboard item arguing that annotation intent must be split into comment, suggestion, and change-request, with change-request producing a proposed diff and an accept/reject surface to remove ambiguity.
intention_for_embedding: To persuade the design to make intent alter the envelope's downstream contract, enabling one-tap resolution of concrete change requests.
